- Ask questions in **Slido** (the link is pinned in chat when live)

Also, don’t post questions in the live chat, since they may be missed if the room is busy.

question: How do I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name there?
answer_llm:
Click the **"Jump to your record on the leaderboard"** link — it will take you directly to your entry.

If you want to see or change the name shown publicly, use **"Edit Course Profile"**.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why do I have a random display name on the course leaderboard, and can I change it?
answer_llm:
You get a random display name automatically when you set up your account, which is why you may see something like “Lucid Elbakyan” on the leaderboard.

Yes, you can change it:
- Go to **Edit Course Profile**
- Change the **first field** to your preferred **display name / nickname** for the leaderboard
- The **second field** is your **official name**, which is what will appear on your certificate

If you want, you can use a Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname for the public leaderboard.

question: Can I take this course on my own pace and still get the certificate, or is it only for the live group?
answer_llm: No. You can only get the certificate if you finish the course with a live cohort. The self-paced mode does not include a certificate because you need to peer-review 3 capstones while the course is running.

No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before the deadline?
answer_llm:
Because the homework may still be in draft. If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means it is not ready yet, so the content can still change until both are true:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform
- the homework title does **not** contain `[DRAFT]`

Until then, working on it in advance is at your own risk because the final version may be different.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You get that error because your OpenAI API account has **insufficient credits / quota**. The course note says there is **no free usage** of the OpenAI API, so you need to **add billing credits** and then try again.

Steps:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account (minimum about $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account in `responses.create(...)`.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you want, I can also help you check whether this is an OpenAI billing issue or an OpenRouter quota issue.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does the insufficient credits error mean in OpenAI responses.create?
answer_llm:
It means your OpenAI account doesn’t have enough remaining API credit/quota to run the request. In the course context, this can appear as:

`OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits`

You should add billing/credits to your OpenAI account and try again.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: How can I fix the OpenAI credits issue when calling responses.create?
answer_llm:
Fix the issue by lowering `max_output_tokens` in your `responses.create()` call. For example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you're using `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method. If you're using ToyAIKit for Q6, pass it like this:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

Also make sure your `.env` is pointing to OpenRouter and that your key has credits:

question: Why is my OpenAI responses.create command failing with an Insufficient credits message?

Your `responses.create` command is likely failing because your account has insufficient credits or quota.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
```

- the model you’re using is valid for your account

question: Why am I getting RateLimitError 429 with OpenAI in this RAG module, and what does it usually mean?
answer_llm:
A `RateLimitError 429` from OpenAI in this RAG module usually means **you’ve exceeded your OpenAI API quota or billing limit**.

In this course context, it often shows up as:

- **`insufficient_quota`**
- a message like **“You exceeded your current quota, please check your plan and billing details”**

So it’s often **not just a temporary rate limit** — it can mean your account has **no remaining API credit** or billing is not set up.

Common fix:
1. Add billing/credits to your OpenAI account
2. Make sure you are using a model available to your account
3. Regenerate/update your API key if needed

If it’s a true rate-limit issue from too many requests per minute, then reducing calls, retrying with backoff, and lowering concurrency can help.


question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv for Python setup?
answer_llm:
The course uses **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It acts as an **all-in-one tool** that can replace tools like `pip`, `virtualenv`, `pip-tools`, `pipx`, `poetry`, `pyenv`, and `twine`, so you don’t have to manage several separate tools.

It also makes it easier to handle:
- Python versions
- virtual environments
- dependencies
- reproducible lockfiles

That said, **you don’t have to use uv** — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine. The lessons just use `uv` because it makes setup **simpler and faster**.
answer_orig:

It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: How do I keep my API keys out of git and load them from a .env file in Python?
answer_llm:
Add your keys to a `.env` file, keep that file out of git, and load it in Python with `python-dotenv`.

### 1) Ignore the file in git
Add this to `.gitignore`:

```gitignore
.env
```

### 2) Create a `.env` file
Example:

```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

### 3) Install `python-dotenv`
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

### 4) Load it in Python
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

That keeps your API keys out of git and lets your Python code read them from the `.env` file.
answer_orig:
Store API keys in a `.env` file and load them with `python-dotenv`, as recommended in the course.

Add `.env` to `.gitignore` so keys are never committed:

```gitignore
.env
```

Create a `.env` file:

```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

Install `python-dotenv` if needed:

```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

Load the keys in Python:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
```

question: How can I use a Google Gemini model in the Module 1 notebook with the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
Use Google Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint by switching the OpenAI client’s `base_url` and using your Gemini API key.

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You need:
1. `GEMINI_API_KEY` in your `.env`
2. `base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"`
3. A Gemini model name like `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`

Note: this uses chat completions, so you may need to adjust notebook code that expects the newer Responses API, especially for tools and response parsing.

question: Why does the notebook need changes if I use the chat completions API instead of the Responses format?
answer_llm:
Because the notebook examples are written for the newer **Responses API**, which reads output differently than **chat completions**.

With **Responses** you’d access things like:

- `response.output`
- responses-style tool/function handling

With **chat.completions** you need to change the notebook to use:

- `response.choices[0].message.content`

And if the notebook uses tools or function calls, those may also need to be adapted to the schema your provider expects.

So the notebook needs changes mainly in the places where it:
1. reads the model’s response, and
2. handles tools/function calls.

If your provider supports chat completions better, it’s okay to use that older API.

question: How do I make Gemini return a Pydantic model if I’m using the OpenAI-compatible endpoint and `responses.parse` is not there?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

Define your Pydantic model, then call:

```python
client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=YourModel
)
```

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This gives you a parsed Pydantic object directly, without manual JSON parsing.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python

```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

```python
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

]

    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama, go to **https://ollama.com/download** and choose your operating system:

- **macOS**: download the `.pkg` file and install it.
- **Windows**: download the `.msi` file and install it.
- **Linux**: run:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it by running:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```
answer_orig:
First, install Ollama by visiting [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and choosing your operating system:

- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

- Download the LLaMA 3 model (~4GB).
- Start the model locally.
- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.

To test the Ollama local server, run the following command:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:

```json
{"models": [...]}  
```

Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

Here is a minimal Python example:

```python
import ollama

response = ollama.chat(
    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

If you stop and restart the notebook cell, make sure to rerun the cell that starts `ollama serve`.

question: Why do I get a different token count with tiktoken than the number OpenAI shows in the API?
answer_llm:
Because `tiktoken` only counts the visible text, while OpenAI’s API also counts hidden chat-formatting tokens.

Specifically:

- Each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- The API adds 2 extra tokens to start assistant generation
- Whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can also change the count slightly

So a prompt that looks like 320 tokens with `tiktoken` might be reported as 327 by OpenAI.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: How can I make my API key load automatically when I enter a project folder, without re-exporting it in every shell?
answer_llm:
Use a tool like **`dirdotenv`** so the key loads automatically when you `cd` into the project folder.

Example:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then in your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the API key will be loaded automatically in that folder, without re-exporting it in every shell.

If you already use **`direnv`**, that also works.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I figure out the right field weights for minsearch or a similar search engine?
answer_llm:
The best way is to **evaluate different field weights against a ground-truth dataset**.

A practical process:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Label which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weight combinations.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like:
   - hit rate
   - precision@k
   - recall@k
   - MRR

For small projects, you can start with **trial and error**, but evaluation is the more reliable method.

question: What’s the best way to clean documents before using them in RAG?
answer_llm:
The best way is to make the documents **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

In practice, that means:

- Remove obvious noise like broken HTML, duplicates, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers, and repeated footers.
- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the cleaned result in a structured format, often JSON.
- Chunk the text so related context stays together.
- Keep metadata that may help with filtering or ranking later.

The main goal is not just to shorten the text, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, why does uv keep saying it failed to hardlink files, and should I worry about it?
answer_llm:
No, it’s not an error you need to worry about. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` sometimes can’t hardlink files between its cache and the target environment, so it prints a warning and falls back to copying the files instead. The package still installs normally.

If you want to suppress the warning in the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make that persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

```bash

```

To make it persistent:

```bash

source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
```

question: If I keep working in Codespaces and never commit, does my code just stay there safely or can it disappear later?
answer_llm:
Your code is saved in the Codespaces Linux VM, but it is **not guaranteed to stay there forever**.

Codespaces can stop, disconnect, be rebuilt, or even be deleted later. So if you never commit, your work may disappear. The safe practice is to **commit and push regularly** so it’s stored in your GitHub repository.
answer_orig:
The code is saved inside the Codespaces Linux VM.

However, you should still commit your work regularly. Codespaces can stop, disconnect, or be deleted later, and committing makes sure your work is stored in your GitHub repository.

question: Where is my code actually stored when I save it in GitHub Codespaces before committing?
answer_llm:
Your code is stored inside the Codespaces Linux VM, in the `/workspaces` environment, until you commit it.

So:
- **Before committing:** it lives in the Codespaces VM
- **After committing:** it’s stored in your GitHub repository

Uncommitted changes usually survive a stop/start, but can be lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more system memory than is available even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 is only using the amount of RAM allocated to WSL, not all the RAM in your PC. So even if your computer has enough total memory, WSL2 may still report less available memory inside the Linux environment.

To fix it, increase the WSL2 memory limit by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

For example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, run `free` inside WSL to check the available memory.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: How do I increase the RAM that WSL2 can actually use on Windows?
answer_llm:
Create a `.wslconfig` file in your Windows user profile folder, for example:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

Add:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, open WSL again and check with `free` to verify the RAM change.

question: Why does my GitHub Codespace keep hanging on Finishing up, and how can I get it to open again?
answer_llm:
This is usually a GitHub Codespaces reliability issue, not a course problem.

Try these steps:

1. Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces)
2. Stop the codespace
3. Start it again

If it still hangs on “Finishing up”:

- Open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code
- Try a different browser, like **Edge, Chrome, or Brave**
- As a last resort, **delete the codespace and create a new one**

If your setup seems to have disappeared, note that:
- Your repo in `/workspaces` usually persists across stop/start
- But a **rebuild** or **new codespace** starts from a clean image, so system/global installs may be gone
- Reinstalling is usually quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`)
- **Commit and push often**, because uncommitted changes are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace

If it keeps being flaky, you can also run the course locally instead.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: My files or setup disappeared in a new Codespace, is that normal or did I lose everything?
answer_llm:
Yes, that can be normal in Codespaces.

A **brand-new codespace starts from a clean image**, so things like **system/global installs** or setup outside your project may look like they disappeared. The **repo in `/workspaces` persists** across stop/start, but if you **deleted/recreated** the codespace or did a **rebuild**, some setup won’t be there anymore.

What to do:
- **Reinstall setup quickly** with `uv` if needed (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`)
- **Commit and push your work often**, because uncommitted changes are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace
- If you’re having lots of Codespaces issues, consider running the course locally instead

If you want, I can also help you figure out whether what disappeared was your **files**, your **packages**, or your **environment setup**.

question: Can I use a provider other than OpenAI for the RAG homework, or is the course tied to it?
answer_llm:
Yes — you can use a provider other than OpenAI. The course isn’t tied to OpenAI.

You can use:
- other hosted OpenAI-compatible providers like Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, or Mistral
- open models via Hugging Face
- a locally served model with Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio, etc.

Usually you just need to change the `base_url`, API key, and model name.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: Why do I keep getting 429 errors when my notebook loops over lots of docs, and what can I do to stop them?
answer_llm:
You’re likely hitting OpenAI’s per-minute and/or per-day rate limits because your notebook is making too many API calls in a loop over many documents. This is especially common on free-tier or low-spend accounts.

What you can do:

- **Reduce API calls before anything else**
  - Don’t loop one-by-one if you can batch inputs.
  - Cache embeddings and model outputs so reruns don’t call the API again for the same docs.

- **Use retries with backoff**
  - The `openai` Python client can retry automatically:
    ```python
    from openai import OpenAI

    client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
    ```

- **Lower concurrency**
  - If you’re using threads/processes, keep it small, like **2–3 workers**.

- **Batch where possible**
  - For embeddings, send a list of texts in one request instead of many separate calls.

- **Use a cheaper/free model/provider**
  - `gpt-4o-mini` is suggested as cheap enough for the course.
  - Groq’s free tier with `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` has generous per-minute limits via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

One important distinction: if the error is `insufficient_quota`, that’s **billing/quota**, not a rate limit issue. In that case you need to add credits to your OpenAI account.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: How can I make OpenAI requests retry automatically instead of failing on a temporary rate limit?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI Python client’s built-in retry support:

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

That makes temporary 429 rate-limit errors retry automatically with exponential backoff instead of failing right away.

question: What’s a good way to keep my embedding or LLM notebook from re-calling the API every time I rerun it?
answer_llm:
A good way is to **save the embeddings/vectors to disk once, then reload them on later runs** instead of recomputing them every time.

The context suggests:

- **Move the embedding step into a separate Python script** you run once from the terminal.
- **Save the resulting vectors**.
- Then **load the saved vectors back into the notebook**.

That avoids re-calling the API every time you rerun the notebook.

question: What exactly counts as a tool or function in agentic RAG, and what is it used for?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool/function** is anything the model can **call for external help** when it needs it.

Examples include:
- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

What it’s used for:
- to let the model **decide when to invoke outside capabilities**
- unlike basic RAG, where retrieval is usually a **fixed step before answering**, in agentic RAG the model can choose to call the tool during the interaction

So in this course, **“tool” and “function” are basically the same idea**.

In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call `responses.create` with a non-OpenAI model?
answer_llm:
Because `responses.create(...)` returns an OpenAI Responses object only for OpenAI’s Responses API. Non-OpenAI providers often return a plain string or use the chat-completions format instead, so there is no `.output` attribute to access.

For non-OpenAI models, use:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

Also, you may need to adapt the tools schema to match what that provider expects.

question: How should I define the weather tool JSON for Azure OpenAI so I stop getting the missing tools[0].function error?
What exactly needs to change in my get_weather_tool object when Azure says parameter tools[0].function is missing?
Can you show the correct function tool format for get_weather in this module?
Why does my Azure OpenAI request fail with Error code 400 and missing required parameter tools[0].function?
What is the proper JSON schema for a weather function tool with a city field?
Why does toyaikit give a 404 error with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, and what should I use instead?
answer_llm:
Because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **only implemented by OpenAI**. So when you point it at Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, you can get a `404`.

What to use instead:

- For **Groq / other OpenAI-compatible providers**: use the **chat completions** versions:
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

- For **Anthropic**: use:
  - `AnthropicClient`
  - `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

So the fix is to switch from the Responses-based runner to the **chat completions** runner for OpenAI-compatible providers, or to the Anthropic-specific runner for Anthropic.
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: What is the difference between OpenAIResponsesRunner and the chat completions runner in toyaikit?
answer_llm:
The difference is:

- **`OpenAIResponsesRunner`** uses OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`).
  - This is **OpenAI-specific**.
  - It can fail with a `404` or similar on non-OpenAI providers like Groq or Anthropic.

- **Chat completions runners** like **`OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`** use the standard **chat completions API** (`chat.completions.create`).
  - This works with **OpenAI-compatible providers** such as Groq.

So, in short:

- **OpenAIResponsesRunner** = OpenAI-only Responses endpoint
- **Chat completions runner** = more portable, works with OpenAI-compatible providers

If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create, and how do I fix it?
answer_llm:
You’re getting a **402 from OpenRouter** because `responses.create()` is being called **without a reasonable `max_output_tokens` limit**.

OpenRouter checks billing/limits against the **maximum possible output**, which can be very large if you don’t set a limit, so a free or low-limit key may be rejected **before the model runs**.

## How to fix it
Set a smaller `max_output_tokens` value, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

## If you’re using `rag_helper.py`
Add the same parameter inside the `llm()` method:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

## If you’re using ToyAIKit
Pass it via `extra_kwargs`:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

## Also check your config
Make sure your `.env` is pointing to OpenRouter correctly:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails, check your OpenRouter key credits/limits and prefer a **pinned model** like `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free` instead of `openrouter/free`.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

   - Use an `sk-or-v1-...` key
   - Check remaining credits / key limits in your OpenRouter settings

2. **Your model is pinned to a specific OpenRouter model**
   - Prefer something like:
     ```env
     OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
     ```
   - Avoid `openrouter/free`, because it can route to models with different limits

Also make sure you set a reasonable `max_output_tokens` like `1024`, because OpenRouter may block requests with very large default output limits before the model runs.

If you want, I can show the exact `.env` and `responses.create()` setup.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: What changes when RAG uses the chunk index rather than the original document list?
answer_llm:
When RAG uses the **chunk index** instead of the original document list, it searches and retrieves from the **smaller chunked pieces** of the documents rather than the full documents.

So the changes are:

- **Indexing happens on chunks, not full docs**  
  `index.fit(chunks)` instead of `index.fit(documents)`

- **Retrieval returns chunks**  
  The system pulls back the most relevant chunk(s), not whole documents.

- **The prompt gets less context**  
  Only the retrieved chunks are inserted into the prompt, which reduces the amount of text sent to the model.

In short: it changes RAG from working with **full documents** to working with **retrieval-ready chunks**, which makes context smaller and retrieval more precise.

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I'm using a model that is not OpenAI-based?
answer_llm:
For Module 1 homework, if you use a non-OpenAI model, get the token count from the model’s response object, not from `tiktoken`.

- Look for fields like `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the provider/API.
- If your provider doesn’t expose usage, use that provider’s native tokenizer as an approximation.
- Don’t use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, etc., because tokenization differs.

If you want, I can help you adapt this to a specific provider.
answer_orig:
For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why does the homework checker give me a 500 or other non-200 error for my repo link, and what should I change?
answer_llm:
The checker is trying to open your submitted repo URL with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `500` or another non-200 code, the link is probably not publicly reachable as-is.

What to change:
- Make the repo **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in `.git`
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Check for a **typo** in the URL

Quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito window. If it loads there, the checker should be able to access it too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do people say they keep the meaning of the original data?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are numerical representations of non-numerical data, such as text, designed to preserve meaning and context.

The idea is:
- convert words, sentences, or documents into vectors of numbers
- if two pieces of text are similar in meaning, their embeddings should be close to each other in vector space
- this closeness lets us use mathematical similarity methods, like dot product or cosine similarity, to find related items

People say embeddings “keep the meaning” because the embedding process tries to place similar content near each other, so the numerical vector reflects semantic similarity, not just raw text matching.

Related ideas:
- vector space model
- dimensionality reduction
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How does an embedding model turn text or other non-numeric stuff into numbers that still stay close for similar inputs?
answer_llm: Embeddings convert non-numeric data, like text, into numbers while preserving meaning and context. The idea is that similar inputs get mapped to similar vectors, so their numerical representations stay close in the vector space.

question: Why are embeddings useful for semantic similarity search, and how does being nearby in vector space help with that?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are useful for semantic similarity search because they convert text or other non-numerical data into vectors in a way that preserves meaning and context. If two inputs are semantically similar, their embeddings should end up close to each other in vector space.

question: Can you explain the link between embeddings, vector space models, and dimensionality reduction in simple terms?
answer_llm:

1. **Embeddings** create the numeric representation.
2. That representation lives in a **vector space model**.
3. The vector space is usually much smaller than the original data, which is a form of **dimensionality reduction**.

question: Why did the lesson use dot product straight away for similarity, but the homework says to normalize first?
answer_llm:

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: For vector search, should I index the question text, the answer text, or both together?
answer_llm:
There’s no single right answer — it’s an experiment on your dataset.

The course suggests trying three options:
- **Question only**
- **Answer only**
- **Question + answer together** (`question + " " + text`)

Often, **question + answer together** works best, but it’s a bit longer and slightly more expensive to embed.

Best practice: compare them on your ground-truth set and pick the one with the best **hit rate / MRR**.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: How does cosine similarity measure how close two vectors are in text search?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors are by looking at the **angle between them**, not their length.

- If two vectors point in the **same direction**, cosine similarity is **1**.
- If they are **unrelated/orthogonal**, it is **0**.
- If they point in **opposite directions**, it is **-1**.

In text search, this means two documents or a query and a document are considered more similar when their vector representations are more aligned, regardless of how long the vectors are.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why do people use cosine similarity with word counts or TF-IDF values?
answer_llm: People use cosine similarity with word counts or TF-IDF values because it measures how similar two documents are based on the angle between their vector representations, rather than their size. This makes it useful for comparing text content when the vectors are built from word counts or TF-IDF values.

- **-1** means the vectors are **completely opposite**

question: Can cosine similarity be used only for non-zero vectors, and why?

In practice, cosine similarity is often used with vectors that are normalized to unit length, which also requires them to be non-zero.

question: How is cosine similarity different from just comparing the raw values of two documents?

- **1** = very similar direction
- **0** = no similarity
- **-1** = opposite direction

If vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is just the **dot product**.

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into just a dot product after normalization?
answer_llm:
Because cosine similarity is

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\|\|y\|}
\]

If you **normalize** both vectors first, then \(\|x\| = \|y\| = 1\). So the formula becomes:

\[
\cos(\theta) = x \cdot y
\]

So after normalization, cosine similarity is just the **dot product**.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: If embeddings are all unit length, why can we use matrix multiplication for search?
answer_llm:
If embeddings are all unit length, then the **dot product equals cosine similarity**. That means you can compare a query vector against all document vectors using a **matrix multiplication** operation, which is much faster than looping over vectors one by one.

So the reason is:

- unit-length vectors remove the need to divide by magnitudes
- cosine similarity becomes a dot product
- dot products against many vectors can be computed efficiently with matrix multiplication

question: What changes in cosine similarity when the query vector is normalized too?
answer_llm:
If the query vector is normalized too, cosine similarity becomes just the dot product between the normalized query and each normalized embedding.

question: For Module 2, am I supposed to make a whole new GitHub repo, or just spin up another codespace from the repo I already have?
answer_llm: Just spin up another codespace from your existing repo — you do **not** need a new GitHub repo for Module 2. Use a separate codespace for the vector search work, since those dependencies are heavier.

question: Why do the dot product and the loop version of vector search not match exactly?
answer_llm:
They don’t match exactly because of **floating-point precision**.

`X.dot(v_query)` and the loop compute the same dot products, but they may add the numbers in a different order. Since floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, you can get tiny differences in the last decimal places. The `dot` version may also use optimized BLAS code internally, which changes the summation order.

So the results are effectively the same; just compare them with a tolerance, e.g.:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Why am I seeing IndexError list index out of range when I try to get the top chunk?
answer_llm:
The `IndexError: list index out of range` usually means your embeddings and chunks are out of sync.

Make sure you generate embeddings from the chunk list itself, so the number of vectors matches the number of chunks:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `len(X)` should equal `len(chunks)`. If it doesn’t, accessing the top chunk can fail.
answer_orig:
The error typically happens when the number of embeddings you generate does not match the number of document chunks. Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: Why is my Q2 cosine similarity not matching any multiple-choice answer in module 2 homework?
answer_llm:
The most likely reason is that you used the wrong embedding model.

For Module 2 homework, you should use:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

and **not** `sentence-transformers` or a lesson model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1`.

Also check that you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field,
- comparing it to the **Q1 query vector**,
- and using the fact that the `Embedder` already returns normalized vectors, so the **dot product is the cosine similarity**.

If it’s still slightly off, pick the **closest option**. Small numerical differences are expected.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily API keys for Kestra before running the module?
answer_llm:
Set them as environment variables **before** starting Kestra, and base64-encode the ones Kestra reads as secrets:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start or restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In flows, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. ```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash

```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:


See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra need both GEMINI_API_KEY and SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY, and what is the difference?
answer_llm:
Kestra needs both because they serve different purposes:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **plain API key**.  
  - It is used by the **Kestra AI Copilot**.
  - Kestra reads it directly from the environment.

- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **base64-encoded secret** version.  
  - It is used by the **flows**.
  - Kestra expects secrets to be stored with the `SECRET_` prefix and encoded in base64.

So the difference is:

- **Plain key** = for the Copilot
- **`SECRET_` key** = for flow secret injection

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra so it picks up the variables.

question: After adding the keys, do I need to restart Kestra for the changes to work?
answer_llm:
Yes — you need to restart Kestra so it picks up the new environment variables.

The context says to export the keys **before** starting Kestra, then run:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

If Kestra is already running, restart it after adding the keys.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help create Kestra flows, and what setting am I missing?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot says it can only help create Kestra flows because it’s not getting a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to that canned message.

What you’re missing is the **plain** environment variable:

```bash
GEMINI_API_KEY
```

Not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

Fix:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also make sure:

- the variable is set in the same shell where you run `docker compose up`
- the key is valid and not mistyped
- you haven’t hit Gemini free-tier rate limits/quota

You may also need to export **both**:
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the flows
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.

question: Do I need to use GEMINI_API_KEY or SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY for the Copilot to work in Kestra OSS?
answer_llm:
Use the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot in Kestra OSS.

`SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is for the flows, not for the Copilot. In practice, you should set both:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra.

question: Could the Copilot error be caused by a bad key or quota limit, and how can I tell?
answer_llm:
Yes — it could be a bad key or a quota/rate-limit issue.

How to tell:
- If the Copilot says it can only assist with creating Kestra flows, that usually means it did not get a valid **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- Check whether the variable is actually set in the same shell you used to start Kestra:
  ```bash
  echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
  ```
- If the key is set but it still fails, the key may be mistyped or rate-limited.
- If you’ve been using the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota, which can show up as:
  `429 Resource Exhausted`

What to do:
- Generate a fresh key in Google AI Studio.
- Export the plain key before starting Kestra:
  ```bash
  export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- Make sure you also have the base64 version for flows:
  `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`

So: yes, a bad/missing key or quota limit are both possible causes, and the shell check plus a 429 error are the main clues.

question: Why does Kestra still say my Gemini key is missing even after I set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Because `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is used by the flows, but the AI Copilot reads the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`.

If Kestra says the Gemini key is missing, common causes are:

- You only set `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` but not `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- Kestra wasn’t restarted after exporting the variable
- The encoded secret is corrupted because of a trailing newline/space

What to do:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

Also make sure your flow references it like this:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

If you want, I can help you check whether you’re using the Copilot or a flow, since they need different variables.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could the base64 secret be wrong if I used echo instead of echo -n before encoding the API key?
answer_llm:
Yes — it could be wrong if `echo` added a trailing newline or space before base64 encoding.

Use:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

If the secret was encoded with plain `echo`, Kestra may decode a slightly corrupted value and the API key can fail.

question: What's the easiest way to load the Gemini key for Kestra, a shell export or a .env file?
answer_llm:
The easiest way for Kestra is to use a `.env` file and pass it to Docker Compose.

That said, for Kestra specifically, the course recommends exporting the variables in your shell before starting Kestra, because it needs:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the AI Copilot
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the flows, base64-encoded

Example:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

If you want the `.env` approach, you can also do:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

So: shell export is the course’s main recommendation for Kestra, but `.env` is a cleaner alternative.

question: Why do we need an orchestrator like Kestra if a notebook can already run the code?
answer_llm:
We need an orchestrator like Kestra because a notebook is fine for experimenting, but real workflows need more than “run once” code.

Kestra helps with:
- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- chaining tasks reliably

It also lets you build production-style AI workflows, not just notebook cells. In this course, Kestra is used to teach AI Copilot, RAG, and AI agents in that orchestration context.

If you want, I can also explain how Kestra differs from a notebook in one simple example.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: What is the point of using Kestra instead of just doing everything in a script once?
answer_llm:
The point is that Kestra gives you more than “run a script once.” It adds:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring/observability
- secret management
- reliable chaining of multiple tasks

So instead of a one-off script or notebook cell, you get a workflow that is easier to run in production and easier to manage over time.

In this course, Kestra is also used to teach AI workflows like:
- generating flows from natural language with AI Copilot
- grounding outputs with RAG
- building AI agents that choose tools to call

If you only need a simple one-time script, that may be enough. But if you want a workflow that runs reliably and can be maintained, Kestra is the better fit.

question: How does Kestra help with things like retries, monitoring, secret handling, and connecting tasks together?
answer_llm:
Kestra helps by taking care of the workflow concerns around your code, including:

- **Retries**: it can rerun failed tasks automatically
- **Monitoring**: it gives you observability into what ran and what failed
- **Secret handling**: it manages secrets like API keys securely via environment variables
- **Chaining tasks together**: it reliably connects tasks into a workflow and runs them in order

So instead of just running a notebook once, Kestra supports production-style workflows with scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and task orchestration.

question: Do I have to use Kestra for the whole LLM Zoomcamp, or is it only for the orchestration module?
answer_llm:
No — you only need Kestra for Module 3 on orchestration. It’s not required for the rest of the LLM Zoomcamp.

For the capstone project, you can use another orchestrator like Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or none at all. A plain Python script is enough for the ingestion-pipeline criterion.
answer_orig:
No. Kestra is the orchestrator the course teaches in Module 3, but it is not a requirement beyond that module's homework.

For the **capstone project** you are not restricted in technology: you can use Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or no orchestrator at all. A plain Python script that ingests and indexes your data is enough for full points on the ingestion-pipeline criterion (a Jupyter notebook with the same steps is worth 1 point instead of 2). See the [project guidelines](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/project.md) for details.

The course uses Kestra in Module 3 because it is the vehicle for the AI techniques being taught — AI Copilot for generating flows, RAG for grounding responses, and AI agents that call tools. Other orchestrators cover the same scheduling/retry/monitoring ground, but they won't map onto those specific lessons, so it's worth running the Kestra flows at least once. If you already know Airflow (or another tool) and want to compare, give Kestra a try for the module and then use whatever fits your project best.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else — see the [supported providers list](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

question: For the final project, can I choose Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or even just a Python script instead of Kestra?
answer_llm:
Yes. For the final project, you can use Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or even no orchestrator at all. A plain Python script is enough for full points on the ingestion-pipeline criterion.

Kestra is only mandatory for Module 3 homework, not for the capstone project.

question: Besides Gemini, what AI providers can I use with Kestra, and does it work with local models too?
answer_llm:
Kestra supports **OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI/Grok, and any OpenAI-compatible provider**.

Yes, it also works with **local models** if they’re served through an OpenAI-compatible server such as **Ollama** or **LM Studio**.

So in short: you can use providers like **Groq** too, as long as they are OpenAI-compatible.
answer_orig:
Kestra's AI plugin is provider-agnostic: it supports OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, xAI, Grok, and any OpenAI-compatible provider, including local models served through Ollama or LM Studio. You swap the provider block in a flow without changing anything else. See the [full list of supported providers](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

The course uses Gemini because it has a generous free tier, but you are free to use any provider. The [awesome-llms list](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo tracks free and free-tier options; Groq is a popular choice because it is OpenAI-compatible and works with both the chat completions and responses APIs.

There are two ways to use AI in Kestra:

- The **AI plugin** (`io.kestra.plugin.ai`) is the generic one. It is the most flexible for switching providers, though new vendor-specific API features take a bit longer to land here.
- The **provider-specific plugins** (e.g. `plugin-gemini`, `plugin-openai`) expose features unique to that vendor, such as Gemini video generation, before they reach the generic AI plugin.

For OpenAI-compatible providers that don't have their own plugin (DeepSeek, Groq, xAI), point the OpenAI plugin at the provider's base URL instead of the default endpoint.

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand?
answer_llm: Yes. You can build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand using the **no-code form editor** or the **AI Copilot**. You can also generate flows from tools like **Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL** and push them into Kestra from your editor.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.
